    Alerts & notifications
    Critical — act now
    DATP Public Comment reading room is live. The City of Colorado Springs Digital Accessibility Transition Plan (Draft 2026), with the full plan embedded as a PDF and twelve structured public comments residents can adapt and submit. Comment portal links live in the reading room.
    Public comment · COS Digital Accessibility Transition Plan · May 2026
    Open the DATP reading room →
    BOCC petition — service plan transparency & algorithmic pricing disclosure. A general resident petition with four narrow asks within existing BOCC authority. Sign and send.
    Civic action · El Paso County BOCC · May 2026
    Open petition letter →
    Veterans' petition to the EPC BOCC. Companion petition for veterans of any branch — service plan transparency and algorithmic pricing protections, framed around military housing harms.
    Civic action · Veterans · El Paso County BOCC · May 2026
    Open veterans' petition →
    LDC update — public comment on service plan transparency. A 300-word comment ready to submit through the El Paso County Land Development Code update process.
    Public comment · LDC update · El Paso County · May 2026
    Open LDC comment →
    Background brief — algorithmic rent pricing in El Paso County. Sourced reference for journalists, policy analysts, and the COS Chamber housing committee: federal antitrust action, local policy options, and the case for BOCC-level service plan transparency.
    Reference · Housing & pricing · El Paso County BOCC · May 2026
    Read background brief →
    HOME-ARP comment window is live — closes May 26, 2026. COS is collecting public comment on the HOME-ARP Allocation Plan amendment via an ArcGIS Survey123 form Esri's own ACR rates as "Partially Supports" on WCAG 2.1 A/AA. A WCAG 2.1 AA Drupal Webform replacement is ready to install — bilingual EN/ES, JSON webhook + staff email + native storage, inline accommodation intake.
    24 CFR 91.105 · ADA Title II · May 4, 2026
    Open the accessible HTML form preview → Open the HOME-ARP bundle README →
    Downtown Funding and Records Search Search BID assessments, DDA grants, TIF reimbursements, board meetings, and financial records in one place.
    Annual adopted budget — C.R.S. 29-1-101 et seq · April 10, 2026
    Open the search portal →
    ADA Title II deadline: April 24, 2026 — 16 days. All local government websites must meet WCAG 2.1 AA. Zero district sites are confirmed compliant on PDF documents.
    HB21-1110 · 28 C.F.R. Part 35 · April 8, 2026
    Open district audit dashboard →
    249 of 284 metro districts have no website. CORA request is the only way a resident can access their own district's records.
    EPC Assessor audit · April 2026
    See district audit →
    WS Districts (WSDM) serves 30+ districts on a single WordPress site. Last calendar entry: 2021. No governance docs. No financials. One compliance letter fixes all 30+ simultaneously.
    Platform audit · April 2026
    Draft compliance letter →
    High priority
    All posted PDFs likely fail WCAG 1.3.1. Meeting minutes, budgets, and audit docs are almost certainly untagged. This is the real HB21-1110 failure — not the homepage.
    ADA document audit · April 2026
    View ADA analysis →
    0 of 8 audited sites post their service plan. C.R.S. §32-1-202 requires it. This is the foundational legal document. Not one district links it publicly.
    Governance gap · §32-1-202
    See audit dashboard →
    Annual audit documents: publicly posted by 0 of 8 districts. Required by §32-1-106. Due to State Auditor by July 31. The clock is running.
    Governance gap · §32-1-106 · due July 31
    See audit dashboard →
    Informational
    2 CORA requests pending response. Military housing numbers and district governance records both awaiting reply. Response window is still open.
    CORA tracker · April 2026
    View tracker →
    Wolfersberger compliance and Bellamese analysis complete. New findings ready for review on developer agreements, compliance history, and parcel-level records.
    Compliance analysis · April 2026
    Read analysis →
    Jurisdiction finder is live. Enter any El Paso County address to find your metro districts, school district, city jurisdiction, and utilities — plain language, no login.
    New · April 2026
    Find my jurisdiction →
    CSPD Blotter — Weekly History is live. 271 incidents pulled directly from the live blotter feed, paired with a Poisson 95% confidence band to flag statistical anomalies week to week. Tabs are full APG-pattern keyboard accessible; every chart has an aria-label.
    New · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
    Open blotter dashboard →
    Lorson Ranch financial dashboard is live. Seven cross-pledged metro districts, $94.2M aggregate audited deficit, IGA money flows, and a per-home tax calculator — sourced from 2026 budgets and 2024 audits. Companion plain-language briefing available as a download.
    New · Special Districts · April 2026
    Open Lorson Ranch dashboard →
    CBI Missing Persons form — accessible rebuild is live. Resolves all seven critical/serious findings from the ADA audit: skip link, live regions, focus management, EN/ES localization, real-time validation messaging. Drops into the existing Drupal Webform infrastructure with optional Claude API routing.
    New · State · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
    Open CBI form rebuild →
    LexisNexis ADAM Program dashboard — accessible rebuild is live. Audit and reference rebuild of the missing-children dashboard: APG-pattern tabs with roving tabindex and full keyboard nav, solid amber focus rings (3:1 against any surface), darker success-green for AA contrast, and "View Poster" placeholders converted from dead anchors to real buttons.
    New · Private Sector · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
    Open ADAM dashboard →
    COS City of Colorado Springs Police Department
    • Translate
    • Accessibility
    • Contact
    1. Home
    2. Police
    3. Transparency, records & data
    4. Crime data & information
    5. Red light camera statistics

    Program data

    Red light camera citations

    Monthly citation counts from Colorado Springs' 20 automated red light enforcement intersections, 2022 through the first quarter of 2026. Vendor data reviewed by CSPD officers before a notice of violation is mailed to the registered owner.

    20 intersections 2022 – Q1 2026 Updated monthly by vendor

    At a glance

    2024, the last complete year reported citywide

    These figures are calculated from the monthly data the city publishes on this page.

    Citations issued

    46,992

    In calendar year 2024 across all 20 intersections. Up from 31,984 in 2022.

    Events → citations

    1 in 3

    Of 134,103 captured violation events, 35% became citations after officer review. Two-thirds were dismissed.

    Top intersection

    16%

    Platte & Chelton alone accounted for 16% of all citywide citations in 2024 — 7,583 of them.

    Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025

    +24%

    January through March citations are running 24% ahead of the same period last year. 2025 was down 9% from 2024.

    Year over year

    Annual citations, 2022 – 2026

    Total citations issued by year. 2026 shows only January through March.

    Citations rose 47% from 2022 to 2024 as new cameras came online, then fell 9% in 2025. Q1 2026 is tracking to exceed 45,000 if the current pace holds. Source totals for 2022, 2023, and 2024 are as published by the city; 2025 is the sum of published intersection totals.

    Revenue & economics

    What the program generates — and what's not shown

    Colorado Springs charges a $75 fine per citation, a non-moving infraction with no points on the driver's license. Here's what that translates to financially.

    2024 gross revenue

    $3.52M

    At $75 per citation × 46,992 citations. The city's 2022 figure was $2.4M; 2026 is pacing to exceed $3.4M if Q1 holds.

    Revenue per event captured

    $26

    Effective rate across all 134,103 events reviewed by CSPD in 2024. Two of three events are dismissed before becoming citations.

    Foregone revenue, 2024

    $6.53M

    Dismissed events that could have been citations at $75 each. The officer review process rejects them as unreadable, exempt, or unclear.

    Program vendor

    Verra Mobility

    Runs 300+ camera programs nationally. NYC pays them $998M over 5 years. CSPD's contract terms and fee structure are not publicly disclosed.

    Monthly gross revenue

    2024 2025 2026 (Q1 only)

    Revenue peaks every year in late summer and early fall — likely a mix of higher traffic volumes, longer daylight hours lengthening amber-light exposure, and tourism. The October 2024 peak of $366,375 was the highest single month recorded.

    What's not in the $75

    The $75 is the base fine only. Actual revenue to the city is higher, and actual cost to residents who are cited can be higher still. The city charges:

    • Online and phone payment surcharges — convenience fees, amount not published on the program page
    • Late penalties if the citation isn't paid on time
    • Personal service fees if a citation is ignored and has to be served in person (roughly $29 under Denver's model)
    • Collections costs if the citation goes to collections

    What the city's share actually is

    CSPD states the program is "fully funded through fines" and that surplus revenue goes to the city's general fund (not CSPD's budget).

    But the actual split between the city and Verra Mobility isn't public. In other cities, Verra charges either flat monthly per-camera fees (~$5,000–7,000/month typical) or a per-citation share. At 20 cameras, that's $1.2M–$1.7M/year off the top.

    To know the real program margin, file a CORA request for the Verra Mobility contract and annual program financials.

    By intersection

    How intersections compare

    Select a year to see the ranking of all 20 intersections by citation count.

    Select year to display

    The darker bar indicates the top intersection for the selected year.

    Monthly detail

    Complete monthly data

    Every intersection, every month, for the selected year. Click a column header to sort.

    Showing 2025 data

    Monthly citations by intersection, 2025

    Dashes mean a camera was offline or not yet installed. Zeros are real zero-citation months.

    Context

    About this data

    What the numbers mean, where they come from, and why some months look strange.

    Violations vs. citations

    A violation event is any time a camera captures a vehicle that may have run a red light. CSPD officers review each event before a citation is issued.

    Roughly one in three events becomes a citation. The rest are dismissed — often because the plate is unreadable, the driver had a legitimate reason (emergency vehicle, funeral procession), or the footage doesn't clearly show a violation.

    Why some months are blank or zero

    Dashes mean the camera was offline or not yet installed. Several intersections came online mid-2022, so their 2022 numbers only reflect the months they were active.

    Zeros in 2024 at S Academy/Airport and several months in 2023 reflect real outages. June 2023 saw a citywide drop to roughly 15% of normal — likely a system-wide processing issue, not a sudden drop in red-light running.

    Lake Ave / E Cheyenne Mtn went offline in late November 2025 and came back online in February 2026, which is why its 2025 total ends low and its 2026 January shows a dash.

    Data caveats

    The city's own note describes this as "preliminary, moment-in-time data" from the vendor. Numbers can change as events continue to be reviewed.

    Some totals on the original page don't match the sum of their monthly values. Totals shown here are computed from the monthly figures, which are the underlying data of record.

    For deeper questions about program operation, fine revenue, or safety outcomes, see the Red Light Safety Camera Program page.

    Colorado Springs Police

    Automated red light enforcement data is published by the Records & Identification Section in partnership with CSPD's traffic enforcement unit.

    We'll email you a plain-language summary with links to the source documents whenever this office posts new public-record material.
